## Appendix 4: Other federal investments in ASD
The following other government departments play a role in addressing ASD, and they offer a multitude of programs that range from research and development, to employment programs, and to tax credits.
- Canadian Institutes of Health Research invested $44 million in ASD-related research between 2012-13 and 2016-17, $16.7 million from 2016 to 2018 to support the Kids Brain Health Network, including research on early detection and diagnosis of autism, on mental health, and on the wellbeing of autistic individuals. They also invested $39.1 million from 2009 to 2019 to support Kids Brain health. The Canadian Institutes of Health Research have also addressed autism in their Health Research Training program, which provided training opportunities to researchers in the autism field. This does not include community-based applied research.
- Employment and Social Development Canada (ESDC) has funded disability programs that support autistic Canadians by providing them with disability benefits and job opportunities, such as the Ready, Willing & Able program, which provided at least $15 million from 2014 to 2017, and $20 million from 2022 to 2025, the Opportunities Fund for Persons with Disabilities, which provided $12 million from 2021 to 2022, the disability component of the Youth Employment and Skills Strategy (YESS), which provided $10 million in 2020, and the Social Development Partnership Program, which provided $4.5M in 2016 to fund nine innovative projects that identify, develop, and test promising practices and tools that promote the social inclusion of people with disabilities. ESDC has also contributed to the development of the Accessible Canada Act, which came to force in 2019 to improve inclusion and accessibility for people with disabilities in a variety of areas such as employment.
- Canada Revenue Agency administers the Disability Tax Credit program and registered disability savings plans. The Disability Advisory Committee was re-established at the end of 2017 by the Government of Canada to advise the Minister of National Revenue and the Canada Revenue Agency on how to administer and interpret tax measures for Canadians with disabilities.
- Indigenous Services Canada does not currently have an ASD-specific program.

## Appendix 5: ASD Strategic Fund: Project description and results
Three projects dedicated to improving skills and access to employment for autistic adults have reached an acceptable number of participants and provided support for employment opportunities through mentorship, training, and placement in British Columbia, Alberta, Ontario, and Quebec, or improved cultural awareness to support employment of Indigenous youth in Saskatchewan.
- The Employment Mentoring Program, delivered by Jake's House in British Columbia, Ontario, and Alberta served 28 youth (up to 24), 19 adults, and 35 service providers during 2020-21. From those participants, 28 (or 38%) autistic youth and adults were able to receive employment opportunities through the Program. It was focused on developing recruitment practices and resources to facilitate the transition of autistic individuals into paid employment opportunities. Participating organizations found the Program flexible, and participants appreciated the training and inclusion at work sites. Several participating organizations have shown interest in continuing the Program and in hiring autistic individuals. All youth and adult participants reported increased knowledge, skills, and an improved sense of wellbeing.
- The Polaris Enterprise initiative delivered, by Giant Steps Montreal in Quebec City, Laval, and Montreal, reached 250 service providers and nine autistic adults. The project supported the transition from school to work by matching individuals with a mentor and a job placement. This project also held an online conference attended by more than by 150 participants. As a result, Loblaws launched an autism-inclusive hiring process in the greater Montreal area that expanded later across Quebec and the rest of Canada in 70 other stores. Other organizations have approached Giant Steps to facilitate inclusive hiring processes. Once partnerships were in place, the projects started placements. All nine participants reported improved confidence and work readiness.
- The Building Block Program, delivered by the Autism Resource Centre, initiated pre-employment skills development for autistic Indigenous youth aged 18 to 30 years in the First Nation communities of Cowessess and Pasqua in Saskatchewan. The program reached 200 participants and 10 service providers, and dedicated the necessary resources and time to build trust with the First Nation communities. The cultural awareness training followed Truth and Reconciliation principles and provided a better understanding of community needs. Lessons learned from engaging with indigenous communities have been shared via a roadmap.
All projects integrated lived experiences by hiring autistic staff or by engaging with autistic people who had previously engaged with the organization. Also, all projects included engagement with Indigenous communities and were mindful of taking the time necessary to fully build trust and address communities' concerns.
Mental health, wellbeing and caregiver support-related projects were strongly affected by the COVID-19 pandemic as they were geared to provide in-person services. Major adaptations, mostly toward virtual delivery, were required to support wellness through physical activity, mental health interventions, and education on sexual health.
- The Healthy Relationship, Sexuality and Autism program delivered by Autism Nova Scotia reached 185 adults and 17 service providers in eight cities and towns across the Atlantic provinces. The program's curriculum included sexual health, relationships and dating, gender, and human development. After the training, 80% of the participants reported improved wellbeing, 93% felt more knowledgeable about what consent is, and 70% believe they can lead a safe and healthy sexual life.
- The Living Life to the Full course was delivered by Autism Ontario to 107 autistic adults and 211 caregivers. Among autistic participants, 20% of respondents to the post-course survey reported decreased anxiety and a 18% reported a decrease in depression from the baseline. The drastic drop in responses to the follow-up surveys at three, six, and 12 months does not allow for the drawing of conclusions on these timelines. The second component of the project, the Mood Walks program, was cancelled due to a lack of program facilitators, which was hindered by the COVID-19 pandemic and associated lockdowns.
- 'La danse pour le mieux-être' program, delivered by les Grands Ballets , offered dance classes and camps for autistic youth and adults (21 to 30 years), older adults (50 and over), and students in Montreal to help with emotional regulation and expression. The project reached about 700 participants. Qualitative data indicated decreased anxiety among participants.
- The Autism Mental Health Promotion project was delivered by York University to improve mental wellbeing and resilience during transitional phases of autistic individuals' lives through several components: An online mindfulness-based intervention where, of the 234 attendants, 38 autistic adults were interviewed and qualitatively reported reduced stress and anxiety. An in-person acceptance and commitment training workshop, where among the 32 caregivers and clinicians attending, some qualitatively reported having developed the knowledge and confidence to lead these exercises in their own workshops. An online mental health literacy guide was co-developed with 29 autistic people, and 4,515 people visited the site where the guide was posted, but downloads are not available. A video series was developed from the Mental Health Literacy Guide and has been viewed 1,312 times in 2021-22. Participants reported a reduction in anxiety and depression. The Kitchen Table Talks program recruited 15 participants, including service providers, autistic adults, and caregivers from Indigenous communities and were facilitated by psychiatrists and community support specialists.
- An online mindfulness-based intervention where, of the 234 attendants, 38 autistic adults were interviewed and qualitatively reported reduced stress and anxiety.
- An in-person acceptance and commitment training workshop, where among the 32 caregivers and clinicians attending, some qualitatively reported having developed the knowledge and confidence to lead these exercises in their own workshops.

The ASD funding program supported a caregiver training project:
The Caregiver Skills Training program delivered by McGill University reached 16 children, 477 adults, seven caregivers, and 390 professionals during 2019-20 and 2020-21. Around 70% of adult participants reported increased knowledge and skills. Some of the professionals trained through a Master Training program delivered services to under-served rural communities. The program was offered virtually at the national level to low-income families with a focus on remote, immigrant, refugee, and Indigenous communities.

## Appendix 6: Autism monitoring data sources
National Autism Spectrum Disorder Surveillance System (NASS)
The NASS collects anonymous case-level or aggregate data to examine and report on ASD prevalence and demographic characteristics in school-aged children. Data collection is not currently ongoing – PHAC is exploring other data sources to address the limitations associated with education data and recommendations outlined in the 2018 NASS report. Data was collected in 2015 and released in 2018.
The NASS collects anonymous case-level or aggregate data to examine and report on ASD prevalence and demographic characteristics in school-aged children. Data collection is not currently ongoing – PHAC is exploring other data sources to address the limitations associated with education data and recommendations outlined in the 2018 NASS report.
Data was collected in 2015 and released in 2018.
Data is limited to the school-age population, ages 5-17 years old, and collects anonymized case-level or aggregated data based on administrative data from health, education or social service sectors. The data source depends on the participating jurisdiction with educational sources being the most common. The case definition applied in educational sources varies depending on the data source used. The NASS ASD Case Definition requires the below criteria to be met: The diagnosis of ASD is provided or confirmed by a licensed health care professional(s) The diagnosis of ASD is based on the clinical criteria in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual for Mental Disorders, or the case is identified as ASD in the International Classification for Diseases. Indicators captured are limited to prevalence. Tracking of time trends is limited in some jurisdictions.
Data is limited to the school-age population, ages 5-17 years old, and collects anonymized case-level or aggregated data based on administrative data from health, education or social service sectors. The data source depends on the participating jurisdiction with educational sources being the most common. The case definition applied in educational sources varies depending on the data source used. The NASS ASD Case Definition requires the below criteria to be met:
- The diagnosis of ASD is provided or confirmed by a licensed health care professional(s)
- The diagnosis of ASD is based on the clinical criteria in the Diagnostic and Statistical Manual for Mental Disorders, or the case is identified as ASD in the International Classification for Diseases.
Indicators captured are limited to prevalence. Tracking of time trends is limited in some jurisdictions.
Canadian Health Survey on Children and Youth (CHSCY) , including ASD-specific data from PHAC's Highlights Report
The CHSCY survey aims to provide information on a regular basis to inform evidence-based policy and program development and support children's health surveillance programs. Data was collected 2019 and released in July 2020. Next collection is planned for 2023.
The CHSCY survey aims to provide information on a regular basis to inform evidence-based policy and program development and support children's health surveillance programs.
Data was collected 2019 and released in July 2020. Next collection is planned for 2023.
CHSCY: Given the design of CHSCY, which covers children and youth - ages 1 to 17 years old living in private dwellings in the 10 provinces and three territories, some populations are excluded from the data collection. These include children and youth living in First Nation reserves Indigenous communities, as well as those living in foster homes and institutions, which is an issue for understanding the specific needs of these populations. Autism data is based on parents' declarations rather than confirmed cases. ASD-specific data published in PHAC's report: Although provinces are covered, estimates cannot be reported for the three territories due to small sample sizes. Disaggregated data is reported for sex-at birth, age and household income. Data on ethnicity, household education, and location (urban or rural) was collected but not reported as no statistical difference was found across categories.

Canadian Survey on Disability (CSD)
"CSD is a national survey that collects information about the lived experiences of youth and adults whose everyday activities may be limited due to a long-term condition or health-related problem." Footnote 42 Data was collected in 2017 and released in 2018. Data collected in 2022 is not yet available.
"CSD is a national survey that collects information about the lived experiences of youth and adults whose everyday activities may be limited due to a long-term condition or health-related problem." Footnote 42
Data was collected in 2017 and released in 2018. Data collected in 2022 is not yet available.
Changes made to the CSD survey between the 2012 and 2017 CSD iterations did not allow a data comparison over time. The CSD survey looks at co-morbidity by asking individuals to identify up to two medical conditions that cause them the most difficulty and, as such, any disability cannot be attributed to ASD alone.
